And the rule was to increase the budget if cost per acquisition was below a certain target. I think it was like $35. Now, I kind of left that, I let it run. The mistake I made is I didn't realize that a CPA or if there's no sale, that counts as a CPA of zero. And actually, we were seeing ad sets which had zero sales, which were coming in at zero CPA, having their budgets increased 20%, 20%, et cetera, and things went a little haywire. So what I would recommend is number one, when you're working with rules, make sure that you are completely confident with what you're doing by running them manually and own them and make sure you know what happens if you do this and do that.

## [42:19 - 45:19]

So again, that's really case by case thing. So what I would say is the big part of the algorithm is the pacing algorithm. So if you are stopping starting ad sets or even answering the day, the pacing part looks at how much spend you have and how many hours are left in the day, especially if you're doing daily budgets. If you're doing lifetime budgets, then it's expanding it out on your lifetime and figuring out how to best spend that money. So what I do say is if you are day parting, then the proper way of doing it is that you run a lifetime budget and then you do your day parting in Facebook. So Facebook knows ahead, as soon as the day starts, I've only got this block of time, it's let's say six hours and I've got this budget to spend and Facebook will do its best to work it out. If you're doing it manually and you're, let's say you're starting an ad at nine o'clock in the morning, that's absolutely fine. But what Facebook will do is calculate from nine in the morning till 12 a.m. Now, if you're starting it at nine a.m. and you plan to stop it at six p.m., that you'll mess up your pacing. And so where Facebook is trying to spread that budget all the way through the rest of the day, you're probably gonna find under delivery, under spend, maybe your performance is not as good as well because also what it does, and you know, part of the clever part, one of the clever parts of the algorithm is it tries to hit people up with impressions at the right time of the day on the right device as well. So if you're trying to override that by day parting, where the algorithm might have seen a nine o'clock start and a 12 o'clock finish, it might say that for this particular audience or this particular person, we need to run this impression at one o'clock in the afternoon because that's when this person is on lunch and you know, they're browsing and things like that. We need to run another impression later on because they're more likely to click. And if you stop the ad too early, you might lose out on that stuff as well. So if you're gonna run day parting properly, it has to be run as a lifetime budget. If you're starting it later, it's not a problem. If you're pausing it sooner than 12 a.m. or 11.59 p.m., then that is a problem as well. So that's something I would say, keep an eye on as well. Great point, Depeche, thank you. The first thing, and one just fun thing that I've heard from one of the guys is his way of using bids. And well, this is also a common thing, but I'm not sure actually haven't ever tried that. But what he says is that the strategy was that he comes into play with a super high bid. That was, let's say if the general bid is like $1 around $1, he just goes into there with $500. And so that Facebook won't set your bid to $500. It would just take the average value and add a cent to it. So he always kind of wins the auction with that over bidding. It's just, I guess it's too risky if there's another like crazy guy like that who would put a thousand. So then you're gonna be, you start competing with some real numbers there. So the analogy I use with this is when you look at eBay, so most people have bidded on something on eBay. If you see a product listed, it will have usually a kind of open price. So in the background, there'll be a price the person wants. So take a given product, they've set their kind of ideal price at, let's say $100. You can start bidding at 50 cents, $1, $5, $10. And then you could be the crazy one that goes in at $75. And what happens is your bid stays at 75, but you only bid just enough above the next person. So if someone just bid at $10, you're going at 12, they'll go in at 14, and you'll automatically get bidded up until you hit your threshold, then someone can beat you. But it goes the other way as well, that if you set your heart on this and you put $150, because you absolutely wanna win this, even though it's only worth $100, if someone then goes and bids $160, you still lost. So that's really the best analogy I've got for Facebook, which is kind of how that works as well. So even if you went in at an auction at $500, and the recommended CPA is $2, or your target is $5, whatever it is, doesn't mean you will pay that, it just means that when Facebook works out it's calculations, and it takes into account things like relevant score, conversion rates of your optimized pixel, where if you're using conversion pixels, it will look at your bounce rate on the page, click through rate, there's lots of things it looks at, then it calculates the overall score. So the score doesn't just look at how much you're putting in terms of money, there's actually a numeric value that comes out, and that's your bidding score. So that's just something to kind of iterate again, that this is why this kind of method sometimes works, but also sometimes where your CPA goes super high. So if you imagine your CPA target is $25,
